title: The Three Unsupervised Tasks
concept: Unsupervised learning splits into three main jobs: clustering (grouping similar things), dimensionality reduction (simplifying data while keeping what matters), and anomaly detection (spotting what doesn't fit).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What are the two supervised learning task types you learned in the last unit?
exercises.explain: Right! Supervised learning splits into regression (predicting a number) and classification (predicting a category).

exercises.prompt: True or false: Just like supervised learning, unsupervised learning is really just one single task.
exercises.explain: False! Unsupervised learning actually covers three distinct jobs: clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection.

cards.title: Three different questions
cards.body: Hand someone a shoebox of 500 unlabeled photos and ask them to make sense of it. They might group similar photos together, pick out the handful of details that matter most, or point at the one photo that looks totally out of place. Unsupervised learning can do all three — and each one is its own distinct job.

cards.title: The three unsupervised tasks
cards.body: Clustering groups similar data points together. Dimensionality reduction simplifies data by keeping only the most important parts. Anomaly detection spots the data points that don't fit the pattern. Same starting point — no labels — but three very different goals.

cards.title: Cleaning out a garage
cards.body: Clustering is putting all the tools in one bin and all the sports gear in another. Dimensionality reduction is realizing you only really use 10 of your 100 items and can ignore the rest. Anomaly detection is noticing the one box that's leaking something it shouldn't.

cards.title: One dataset, three views
cards.body: A store's purchase data can be run through all three tasks: clustering reveals customer groups like bargain hunters and big spenders, dimensionality reduction shrinks hundreds of tracked details down to the handful that actually matter, and anomaly detection flags a purchase that looks nothing like the rest — maybe a stolen card.

cards.title: Unsupervised learning isn't one task
cards.body: It's easy to assume "unsupervised learning" means one specific thing. In reality, it's an umbrella covering three separate jobs, each solving a different problem, and each with its own dedicated tools.

cards.title: Group everything vs. flag the outlier
cards.body: Two of the three tasks pull in opposite directions:

left.text:
Groups similar points together
Everything belongs somewhere

right.text:
Looks for the odd one out
Flags what doesn't belong

exercises.prompt: A model groups thousands of shoppers into a handful of groups with similar buying habits. Which unsupervised task is this?
exercises.explain: Right! Grouping similar data points together is exactly what clustering does.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Dimensionality reduction's goal is to keep only the most important parts of the data and drop the rest.
exercises.explain: True! Dimensionality reduction simplifies data down to what actually matters.

exercises.sentence: The unsupervised task that spots data points which don't fit the pattern is called ___ detection.
exercises.explain: Anomaly detection is the task built to flag data points that break the expected pattern.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is an example of dimensionality reduction?

options.text: Shrinking a dataset of 200 tracked details about a car down to the 5 that matter most for its price
Grouping songs into clusters of similar mood and tempo
Flagging a single transaction that looks nothing like the rest

exercises.explain: Correct! Compressing many features down to the few that carry the most signal is dimensionality reduction.

exercises.prompt: A factory camera scans thousands of identical parts and flags the one that looks scratched. Which unsupervised task is this?
exercises.explain: Right! Spotting the one item that breaks the pattern is anomaly detection.

exercises.prompt: A hospital has patient data with 300 tracked measurements per patient, no labels of any kind, and wants to (1) find natural groups of similar patients, and (2) reduce those 300 measurements down to the handful that carry the most useful information. Which two unsupervised tasks does this call for?

options.text: Clustering for the groups, dimensionality reduction for shrinking the measurements
Anomaly detection for the groups, clustering for shrinking the measurements
This requires supervised learning since it involves patients

exercises.explain: Exactly! Clustering finds natural groups, while dimensionality reduction is built specifically to simplify a large number of features down to the important ones.

exercises.prompt: A security team wants a system that watches network traffic and flags any single connection that behaves nothing like the millions of normal connections around it. A teammate says, "That's clustering, since we're comparing data points to each other." Why is the teammate wrong?

options.text: Because the goal is to flag the one point that breaks the pattern, not to sort everything into groups — that's anomaly detection
Because clustering can only be used on customer data
Because network traffic can't be analyzed without labels
Because clustering and anomaly detection are actually the same task with different names

exercises.explain: Exactly! Clustering sorts everything into groups where every point belongs somewhere. Anomaly detection specifically looks for points that don't belong — that's the goal here.
